perturbation.original_text: i) “Confidential Informati on” refers to the following items Discloser discloses to the Recipient: (1) Any information that is in written, graphic, machine readable , or other tangible form, and that Discloser designates as “ Confidential, “specifically including, but not limited to , the BWC Information Systems Documentation containing file specifications and data definitions for files and reports which will be utilized under the contract to be awarded under RFP CSP903918 ; (2) Any oral or visual information Discloser designates as “Confidential” at the time of disclosure; (3) Any source code and any names of actual or potential Discloser s, whether or not marked as confidential; and (4) Any other nonpublic, sensitive or third party information that is possession of Discloser a nd is disclosed to Recipient under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: i) “Confidential Information” refers to the following items Discloser discloses to the Recipient: (1) Any information that is in written, graphic, machine readable, or other tangible form; (2) Any oral or visual information Discloser provides; (3) Any source code; and (4) Any other information disclosed to Recipient under this Agreement. Regardless of whether the Discloser marks it as 'Confidential'.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that the discloser designates the information as 'Confidential' for it to be considered as such, and stating that ALL information becomes confidential, the contract broadens the definition of Confidential Information beyond what is legally reasonable or enforceable, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws which require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 - Definition of trade secret
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 because it eliminates the requirement for the Discloser to designate information as confidential, potentially including information that isn't actually a trade secret or for which reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy haven't been made. Ohio law defines trade secrets as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified contract could encompass information not meeting these criteria, unduly restricting the Recipient's use of publicly available or non-sensitive information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.61 | Uniform trade secrets act definitions. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF As used in sections 1333.61 to 1333.69 of the Revised Code, unless the context requires otherwise: (A) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The modified contract broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by eliminating the requirement for the Discloser to designate it as such. This contradicts Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 as explained by the LLM, which defines trade secrets as information deriving economic value from not being generally known and subject to efforts to maintain secrecy. By encompassing all information, regardless of its trade secret status or efforts to maintain secrecy, the contract potentially restricts the Recipient's use of publicly available or non-sensitive information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Section 1333.61 of the Ohio Revised Code, defines 'Improper means' and 'Misappropriation' in relation to trade secrets, implying that there is an expectation of specific criteria for information to qualify as a trade secret and thus be protected. The modified contract deviates from this expectation, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall not disclose Confide ntial Information to any employe e or subcontractor of Recipient un less such person needs access in order to facilitate the evaluation and preparation of a response to RFP CSP903918 and executes a nondisclosure agreement with the Recipient with terms no less restrictive tha n those of this Section 2)b)
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall not disclose Confidential Information to any employee or subcontractor of Recipient unless such person executes a nondisclosure agreement with the Recipient with terms no less restrictive than those of this Section 2)b).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'need to know' clause and stating that any employee can have access to confidential information so long as they sign an NDA, the contract conflicts with the standard of 'reasonable measures' required under trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts 18 U.S.C. § 1839, which defines 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets. Granting access to any employee or subcontractor, regardless of their actual need for the information, undermines the 'reasonable measures' standard necessary to maintain trade secret protection. This makes the agreement potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the 'need to know' clause for employees to access Confidential Information, only requiring an NDA. This contradicts the standard of 'reasonable measures' required under trade secret law. The provided snippet of 18 U.S.C. § 1839 defines terms related to the protection of trade secrets, and the law explanation suggests the change undermines 'reasonable measures'. By allowing access regardless of need, the modified contract weakens protections and could render the agreement unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient affirms that it sha ll not and shall not allow others to take the Discloser’s Confidential Information outside the United States without express written authorization from the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient affirms that it shall not and shall not allow others to take the Discloser’s Confidential Information outside of Ohio without express written authorization from the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the movement of data to being within Ohio, the contract introduces a geographical limitation that could conflict with federal laws governing interstate commerce and data transfer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: U.S. Constitution, Article I, Section 8, Clause 3 (Commerce Clause)
perturbation.law_citation: U.S. Constitution, Article I, Section 8, Clause 3
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text could potentially contradict the Commerce Clause of the U.S. Constitution (Article I, Section 8, Clause 3) which grants Congress the power to regulate commerce among the several states. By restricting the movement of Confidential Information outside of Ohio, the contract could be interpreted as an undue burden on interstate commerce, particularly if the information is necessary for legitimate business operations that extend beyond Ohio's borders. This is particularly true given the modern realities of cloud computing and remote work, where data often crosses state lines seamlessly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Commerce Clause | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Commerce Clause The Commerce Clause refers to Article 1, Section 8, Clause 3 of the U.S. Constitution , which gives Congress the power “to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among states, and with the Indian tribes.” Congress has often used the Commerce Clause to justify exercising legislative power over the activities of states and their citizens, leading to significant and ongoing controversy regarding the balance of power between the federal government and the states. The Commerce Clause has historically been viewed as both a grant of congressional authority and as a restriction on the regulatory authority of the States. The Constitution does not explicitly define the word “commerce” leading to wide debate as to what powers section 8, Clause 3 grants congress. Some argue that it refers simply to trade or exchange, while others claim that the framers of the Constitution intended to describe more broadly commercial and social intercourse between citizens of different states. Courts have generally taken a broad interpretation of the commerce clause for much of United States history. In 1824’s Gibbons v. Ogden , the Supreme Court held that intrastate activity could be regulated under the Commerce Clause, provided that the activity is part of a larger interstate commercial scheme. In 1905’s Swift and Company v. United States , the Supreme Court held that Congress had the authority to regulate local commerce, as long as that activity could become part of a continuous “current” of commerce that involved the interstate movement of goods and services. For a brief period between 1905 and 1937, the Supreme Court narrowed their interpretation of the Commerce Clause in what has now become known as the Lochner era . Courts during this era experimented with the idea that the Commerce Clause does not empower congress to pass laws which impede an individual’s right to enter a business contract. However, beginning with NLRB v. Jones & Laughlin Steel Corp in 1937, the Court began to recognize broader grounds upon which the Commerce Clause could be use
perturbation.justification: The modified text restricts the movement of confidential information to within Ohio, which potentially contradicts the Commerce Clause (Article 1, Section 8, Clause 3) of the U.S. Constitution. The Commerce Clause grants Congress the power to regulate commerce among the states. By limiting the movement of information to Ohio, the contract could be seen as an undue burden on interstate commerce if that information is needed for business operations that cross state lines. The provided snippet from the Legal Information Institute defines the Commerce Clause as giving Congress the power “to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among states, and with the Indian tribes.” Therefore, restricting the flow of information to a single state interferes with interstate commerce, creating a legal contradiction.
